coredata.dc:title: Hybrid ternary nanocomposite of N-doped carbon quantum dots@SnO2/multiwall carbon nanotubes: A robust and sensitive electrocatalyst for the detection of antineoplastic agent gallic acid
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Muthusankar, Ganesan
Devi, Ramadhass Keerthika
Chen, Shen-Ming
Huang, Yu-Feng
Gopu, Gopalakrishnan
coredata.dc:description: Herein we report the detection of an antineoplastic molecule, gallic acid (GA), by utilizing a hybrid nanocomposite based on N-doped carbon quantum dots decorated tin oxide with multiwall carbon nanotubes (N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT) for the first time. Initially, the hydrothermal approach was used to synthesize an N-CQD@SnO2 nanocomposite, which further ultrasonically hybridized with MWCNTs to produce the hybrid nanocomposite. Then, the physiochemical properties of the hybrid nanocomposite were characterized using various spectroscopic techniques. The presence of N-CQDs in the SnO2 matrix act as an anchoring point for the hybridization of MWCNTs. The incorporation of N-CQDs in the SnO2 matrix and its hybridization with MWCNT resulted in an extremely low charge transfer resistance for electron transport in the hybrid nanocomposite. Then, using the voltammetric methods, we investigated the analytical performance of an N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT nanocomposite modified glassy carbon electrode towards the electrochemical detection of GA. The proposed sensor demonstrates excellent analytical results for GA detection, with a low detection limit (0.042 µM), high sensitivity (3.96 µA µM−1 cm−2), good reproducibility, and repeatability. In addition, the sensor was tested with various tea samples, and the observed decent recovery results confirmed its practical usefulness.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot
Tin oxide
Carbon nanotube
Gallic acid
Electrochemical sensor

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of SnO2 nanoplates and N-CQD@SnO2 nanocomposite
Preparation of N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT hybrid nanocomposite and electrode modification
Structural characterization of nanocomposite & hybrid nanocomposite
EIS and CV studies on the modified electrode
Electrocatalytic performance of N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT/GCE towards CA determination
Effect of scan rate and pH
Interference studies, reproducibility, repeatability, and stability

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV-Vis (Insert: photograph of N-CQDs under daylight (left) and UV lamp (right, at 365 nm)), (b) FI-IR, (c) XRD, (d) HR-TEM image, (e) SAED pattern and (f) EDS spectrum of N-CQDs.

(a) XRD pattern and (b) FT-IR spectrum of SnO2 NPs, and N-CQD@SnO2 nanocomposite. HR-TEM image of N-CQD@SnO2 nanocomposite at the magnifications of (c) 100 nm, (d) 50 nm, and (20 nm). (e) EDS spectrum of N-CQD/SnO2 nanocomposite.
(a, b) HR-TEM images of N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT hybrid nanocomposite at different magnifications (c) SAED pattern of the hybrid nanocomposite.

In 5 mM of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- containing 0.1 M KCl solution, (a) the EIS curves of different modified electrodes at the frequency range from 0.1 Hz to 100 kHz (Insert: Randels circuit model) (b) CV responses of different modified electrodes at 50 mV s−1 (c) CV responses of N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT/GCE at the different scan rate from 10 to 100 mV s−1 and (d) the corresponding linear plot of redox current vs. square root of the scan rate.

(a) comparison of CV responses of bare and modified electrodes for the electro-oxidation of GA (200 µM) in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7) at the scan rate of 50 mV s−1, (b) Bar diagram for the GA oxidation peak (O2) current response for different electrodes. (c) Proposed mechanism for the electrochemical oxidation reactions of GA at the modified electrode surface.

(a) CVs of different scan rates (10–120 mV s−1) at the surface of N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT/GCE with the presence of GA (100 µM) in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7) (b) linear plot of redox current vs. square root of the scan rate (c) CVs of N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT/GCE at different pH from 3 to 11 (phosphate buffer) in the existence of 200 µM GA and (d) calibration plot for pH vs. Epa and Ipa.

(a) DPVs of N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT/GCE at increasing concentration GA (a to t = 0.05–360 µM) and (b) calibration plot for peak current vs. GA concentration., and (c) relative error percentage of DPV responses of N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT/GCE for GA detection in the addition of 100-fold excess concentration of interfering molecules.

DPV signals of GA to the (a) reproducibility study at five modified electrodes (b) repeatability tests of the N CQD@SnO2/MWCNT/GCE at one electrode (c) storage stability tests of the sensor for 24 h and (d) CVs of 25 continuous cycles for the operationa stability of N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT/GCE with 200 µM GA in PBS (pH 7) at the scan rate of 50 mV s−1 .
Schematic illustration for the synthesis of the hybrid nanocomposite.
Comparison of analytical parameters for the determination of GA with reported sensing protocols.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: NPGA - nitrogen-doped porous graphene aerogel, PEI - polyethyleneimine, PDDA - poly(diallyl dimethylammonium chloride), CCE/mRGO - carbon ceramic electrode/mechanically reduced graphene oxide, Gel-RGONS - gelatin functionalized reduced graphene oxide nanosheet, TNrGO - titanium nitride with graphene oxide.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of GA in real samples using N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT/GCE.

head.ce:title: Hybrid ternary nanocomposite of N-doped carbon quantum dots@SnO2/multiwall carbon nanotubes: A robust and sensitive electrocatalyst for the detection of antineoplastic agent gallic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Industrial Chemistry, Alagappa University, Karaikudi – 630001, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Industrial Chemistry, Alagappa University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Industrial Chemistry, Alagappa University, Karaikudi – 630001, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering and Biotechnology, National Taipei University of Technology, No. 1, Section 3, Chung-Hsiao East Road, Taipei 106, Taiwan, ROC

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Engineering and Biotechnology, National Taipei University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering and Biotechnology, National Taipei University of Technology, No.1, Section 3, Chung-Hsiao East Road, Taipei 106, Taiwan, ROC

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, National Taipei University of Technology, Taiwan, ROC

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanical Engineering, National Taipei University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical Engineering, National Taipei University of Technology, Taiwan, ROC

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein we report the detection of an antineoplastic molecule, gallic acid (GA), by utilizing a hybrid nanocomposite based on N-doped carbon quantum dots decorated tin oxide with multiwall carbon nanotubes (N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT) for the first time. Initially, the hydrothermal approach was used to synthesize an N-CQD@SnO2 nanocomposite, which further ultrasonically hybridized with MWCNTs to produce the hybrid nanocomposite. Then, the physiochemical properties of the hybrid nanocomposite were characterized using various spectroscopic techniques. The presence of N-CQDs in the SnO2 matrix act as an anchoring point for the hybridization of MWCNTs. The incorporation of N-CQDs in the SnO2 matrix and its hybridization with MWCNT resulted in an extremely low charge transfer resistance for electron transport in the hybrid nanocomposite. Then, using the voltammetric methods, we investigated the analytical performance of an N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT nanocomposite modified glassy carbon electrode towards the electrochemical detection of GA. The proposed sensor demonstrates excellent analytical results for GA detection, with a low detection limit (0.042 µM), high sensitivity (3.96 µA µM−1 cm−2), good reproducibility, and repeatability. In addition, the sensor was tested with various tea samples, and the observed decent recovery results confirmed its practical usefulness.

ce:section.ce:para: Gallic acid (3,4,5-trihydroxy benzoic acid, GA) is a phenolic compound naturally existing in black tea, green tea, grapes, and many plants [1]. A quick and sensitive determination of GA has a great significance for human health. Because it is not only possessing antiviral, antiallergic, antioxidant properties but also exhibits a potential cytotoxic activity against cancer cells without affecting healthy human cells [2]. Moreover, the kinetics and useful health effects can be evaluated by determining the concentration of GA in body fluids [3]. In industries, GA has been used as an indicator of the validity of fruit juices and alcoholic beverages [4]. Accordingly, the quantitative determination of GA concentration in medicinal plants, human fluids, and foods is essential for pharmacological and life science research. In recent years numerous techniques for determining GA, including spectrophotometry, chromatography, resonance light scattering, capillary electrophoresis, and electrochemical methods, have been developed. Among them, the electrochemical technique has been receiving significant attention due to its simplicity and its efficient determination of GA [5–7]. Since the electrochemical responses of GA occur on the surface of the electrode, thus the choice of electrode modifiers becomes an essential and challenging task for efficient and sensitive determination.

Nowadays, semiconductor-carbon heterostructure materials are picking up extraordinary research focus owing to their unique characteristics and viable appropriateness in energy storage, energy conversion, and sensor applications [8]. Carbon nanomaterials combined with semiconducting transitional metal oxides can attain unique structural features due to the new heterostructure, which is very advantageous for feasible applications. Tin oxide (SnO2), one of these metal oxides, has been extensively researched as n-type semiconducting material. SnO2 is a non-toxic, low-cost substance, and has a more positive conduction band edge than TiO2 and ZnO [9], making it a superior electron acceptor. Moreover, having low chemical potential, makes the SnO2 a desirable electrode material for an electrocatalytic reaction, and an efficient choice in a fast-redox process. Because of these advantageous features, SnO2 is an excellent host material for electrochemical applications [10]. Further incorporation of carbon-nanomaterials (graphene [11], functionalized graphene [12], graphene oxide [13], carbon nanotubes [14]) with SnO2, can provide an improved mechanical buffer and electrochemical characteristics for the detection applications.

In this concern, carbon quantum dots (CQD) are a promising carbon nanostructure and a rising star in the family of carbon nanomaterials. Because of the quantum confinement phenomenon, it has both optical and electrical characteristics. In addition, beneficial properties such as less toxicity, biocompatibility, and chemical stability are also exhibited by the CQDs. As a result, it has been used as a potential candidate in applications such as bio-imaging, optoelectronics, surface-enhanced Raman scattering, electro, and photocatalysis [15–19]. Recent research has demonstrated that nitrogen (N) doping in CQDs (N-CQDs) has been shown to boost the electrocatalytic performances of CQDs [20,21]. Thus, it is envisaged that the incorporation of N-CQDs into the nanostructures will boost electrocatalytic performance. Thus, the current effort aims to construct a hybrid nanocomposite based on N-CQDs adorned SnO2 with multiwall carbon nanotubes (N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT) to improve the sensitivity towards GA detection. N-CQDs are essential in the fabrication of this hybrid nanocomposite. N-CQDs feature a graphitic π conjugated core and the many functional groups on the edges. Due to this fact, the N-CQDs form π-π interactions with MWCNTs, resulting in a stable hybrid nanocomposite. Because of the mechanical strength, high surface area, and good electrical conductivity of MWCNTs [22,23], they have been also used to construct the hybrid nanocomposite in this work. Based on these perspectives, we have integrated the nanostructured SnO2 with N-CQD and MWCNTs to design a nanohybrid material via a simplistic approach for the first time and utilized it as an electrode modifier for the sensitive determination of GA.

Analytical grade reagents of tin chloride (SnCl2·H2O), L-glutamic acid (C5H9NO4), ethylenediamine (EDA, C2H4(NH2)2), and gallic acid (GA, C6H2(OH)3COOH) were acquired from Sigma Aldrich, India and utilized as the received condition. NaH2PO4 and Na2HPO4 were used to make 0.1 M phosphate buffer solutions (PBS) with varying pH conditions, and the pH values were modified with NaOH or HCl. If not utilized, the GA stock solution was made with ethanol and kept at a cold temperature. The remaining solutions were all made using double distilled water.

A homogeneous mixture of L-glutamic acid (2 g) and EDA (15 mL) were mixed in 50 mL of double distilled water and agitated for 30 min. The mixture solution was then autoclaved and subjected to the heat treatment at 180 °C for 6 h. The resulting reddish-brown colored solution was ultracentrifuged (10000 rpm) to remove the larger particles. Finally, the supernatant solution was filtered using a syringe filter (pore size 0.22 µm) to get pure pale-yellow colored N-CQD solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of SnO2 nanoplates and N-CQD@SnO2 nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: With a few changes, the preparation procedure was adopted from the literature reports [24]. A 0.5 M of SnCl2. 2 H2O was prepared in deionized water (10 mL) and stirred well. To this solution, CTAB (15 mM) was added, and the solution pH was adjusted to 8.0 with NaOH solution to get the Sn(OH)2 precipitate. Following that, 10 mL of N-CQD solution was added and stirred for 30 min. After centrifugation, the precipitate was rinsed with double distilled water and ethanol, and dried at 60 °C. The dried product was mortared well before being calcined at 600 °C for 4 h to produce the N-CQDs adorned SnO2 nanocomposite (N-CQD@SnO2). With the exception of N-CQDs, pristine SnO2 was also prepared with the aforementioned procedure for comparison purposes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT hybrid nanocomposite and electrode modification
ce:section.ce:para: About 5 mg of N-CQD@SnO2 nanocomposite and 5 mg of MWCNT were disseminated in 1 mL of isopropanol and ultrasonically irradiated for 1 h using a bath sonicator. After treatment, a black-colored solution was formed, which was dubbed N-CQD@ SnO2/MWCNT hybrid nanocomposite. Using a drop cast method, an optimal volume of 5 μL of the black-colored slurry was coated on the cleaned surface of the GCE (3 mm Dia.). The resulting N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT hybrid nanocomposite modified GCE (N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT/GCE) was then allowed to dry at ambient temperature. Scheme 1 depicts the synthesis steps of the hybrid nanocomposite.

Powder X-ray diffraction (P-XRD) patterns of the materials were recorded (XPERT – PRO; PANalytical B.V., Netherlands diffractometer) in Cu K radiation (= 1.5406) from 2θ of 20–80°. A Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Bruker IFS28) was used to determine the functional groups of samples. The morphology of the samples was examined using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, JEOL JEM-2100 F), and the elemental analysis was performed using the corresponding energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS).

CHI 400 and 900 electrochemical workstations were used to perform electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), differential pulse voltammetry (DPV), and cyclic voltammetry (CV). A three-electrode setup was employed, which included a modified/un-modified GCE as the working electrode, an Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode, and a platinum wire as the counter electrode. Electrochemical experiments were carried out in PBS (pH 7.0) with inert gas (N2). In a 0.1 M KCl solution containing 5 mM of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- redox probe, the electrochemical behavior of the modified electrodes was studied using cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The CV measurements were taken at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 between the potential window of − 0.4 to 0.8 V. The EIS profiles, on the other hand, are documented under open circuit potential with a frequency range of 100 mHz to 100 kHz and an amplitude of 50 mV. The electrocatalytic behavior of modified electrodes towards analyte was investigated using CV and DPV methods. The CVs and DPVs were recorded at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 in an optimum potential window from − 0.2 to+0.5 V using PBS as a buffer solution (pH 7.0).

Under UV light (365 nm, Insert Fig. 1a), the synthesized N-CQDs emitted a bright blue emission. The UV-Vis spectra of N-CQD (Fig. 1a) exhibit a strong peak at 285 nm, which corresponds to the sp2 hybridized carbon system’s π − π * transition. The n- π * transition causes a slight hump at 351 nm, indicating the existence of nonbonding electrons carrying functional groups such as carbonyl (-CO, -C-O-) and hydroxyl groups (-OH) [25]. The N-CQDs FT-IR spectra (Fig. 1b) reveal a characteristic merged peak at 3224, and 3355 cm−1 corresponds to -NH and -OH functional groups, respectively [26]. However, the intensity of the -NH group is low, indicating that there are just a few -NH groups in N-CQDs. Furthermore, the peaks at 1288, 1637, and 1794 cm−1 are assigned to the epoxy (-C-O-C-), in-plan stretching of carbon-carbon double bond (-CC-), and carbonyl (-CO) functional groups respectively. These surface functional groups of N-CQDs make it highly soluble in water. The XRD pattern of N-CQD (Fig. 1c) reveals a large peak at ~ 23°, which is aligned with the (002) plane of the graphitic structure and indicates its amorphous nature [27]. The N-CQD’s measured interlayer spacing (d) is 0.24 nm, which is consistent with the SAED pattern of the N-CQDs seen in Fig. 1e.

The HR-TEM image in Fig. 1d shows nanosized N-CQDs with diameters ranging from 6 to 10 nm. Further, it confirms the partial crystalline nature with a d spacing of 0.29 nm, which may attribute to the graphite plane [28]. The SAED pattern (Fig. 1e) further reveals that N-CQDs are semi-crystalline and amorphous in nature. The EDS analysis (Fig. 1f) confirms the presence of C, O, and N in the N-CQDs. The other element Si has emerged from the glass plate substrate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of nanocomposite & hybrid nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para:
The phase and crystal structure of the samples were studied using XRD. The XRD pattern of pristine SnO2 NPs, and N-CQD@SnO2 nanocomposite is shown in Fig. 2a. The detected crystallographic planes of (110), (101), (200), (211), (220), (002), (310), (112), (202), (321), and (320) in N-CQD/SnO2 nanocomposite and pristine SnO2 are well-matched with the standard data of tetragonal rutile structure (JCPDs #00-041-1445; space group P
42
/mnm; unit cell parameters a, b = 4.738 Å, and c = 3.187 Å). Furthermore, the wide diffraction peak in N-CQD in the N-CQD/SnO2 nanocomposite can be indexed at 20º, which is in good agreement with the effective integration of the N-CQD on the surface of the SnO2 NPs. Furthermore, there is no evidence of an impurity peak, indicating that the samples have exceptional crystallinity. The Scherrer formula (Eq. 1) was used to calculate the average crystallite size.
(1)
D = 0·9λ/β cos θ
Where λ (= 1.5406) is the Cu Kα radiation wavelength, β is the full width at half maximum (FWHM) in a radian of the peak with (hkl) value, θ is the diffraction angle. The N-CQD/SnO2 nanocomposite’s average crystallite size was determined to be 23.5 nm. The FT-IR peaks (Fig. 2b) about 3300 cm−1 and 1630 cm−1 in pristine SnO2, and N-CQD/SnO2 nanocomposite correspond to the stretching and deformation modes of -O−H functional group, respectively. A slight hump around 1100 cm−1in both SnO2 and N-CQD@SnO2 nanocomposite corresponds to the presence of oxygen on the surface (Sn−Osurf). Further, the metal oxide (SnO2) asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations were spotted at 630 and 507 cm−1, respectively [29]. In contrast, N-CQD@SnO2 nanocomposite exhibited a higher frequency of 665, and 528 cm−1, for the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations, respectively. It may be due to the increased force constant caused by the introduction of N-CQDs to the SnO2 matrix.

As a consequence, it has been confirmed that the N-CQD interacts with SnO2 through its numerous functional groups. Thus, it might be caused for the shifting of Sn−O−Sn asymmetric/symmetric stretching vibrations towards the higher frequency. HR-TEM was used to examine the morphological properties of N-CQD@SnO2 nanocomposite. HR-TEM images of N-CQD@SnO2 nanocomposite at various magnifications are shown in Fig. 2c–e. The N-CQD@SnO2 nanocomposite has a spherical shape and a consistent size distribution. The particle size is similar to the estimated crystallite size from the XRD, and lattice lines show that rutile-type SnO2 has a good crystalline structure. The HR-TEM images further reveal that the black dots observed spread around the SnO2 surface are N-CQDs. Thus, it validates the effective inclusion of N-CQDs on the SnO2 NPs. The related EDS spectrum (Fig. 2f) shows the presence of Sn, O, C, and N in the nanocomposite and also confirms the formation of N-CQD/SnO2 nanocomposite.

HR-TEM results of N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT hybrid nanocomposite at various magnifications are shown in Fig. 3a and b. The presence of black spheres on the surface of MWCNTs indicates the N-CQD@SnO2 nanocomposite has been properly attached to the MWCNTs. Moreover, the N-CQD@SnO2 nanocomposite is evenly distributed across the surface of MWCNT. Further, the SAED pattern (Fig. 3c) exhibits both bright spots and ring patterns, which correlate to the crystalline nature of N-CQD@SnO2 nanocomposite and the amorphous nature of MWCNTs, respectively. As a result, it confirms the successful formation of N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT hybrid nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: EIS and CV studies on the modified electrode
ce:section.ce:para:
Charge transport efficiency at the electrode/electrolyte junction is a critical measure for electrochemical sensors. So, the EIS technique was employed to investigate the surface characteristics of the modified electrodes. The impedance spectra were obtained in the frequency range of 10 mHz to100 kHz, as shown in Fig. 4a. The EIS spectra of modified electrodes exhibit smaller semi-circles than the bare GCE. Because the diameter of the semi-circle in the EIS spectrum is directly replicated the charge transfer resistance value. But for the case of MWCNT/GCE, and N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT/GCE exhibits almost a straight line, which indicates that each material modification minimizes the charge transfer resistance. Particularly, the N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT/GCE shows a very negligible amount of R
ct value. The observed enhancement in the charge transport nature of N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT/GCE is nicely connected to the idea that the surface modification of SnO2 with N-CQD and further hybridization with MWCNTs can increase the hybrid nanocomposite’s conductivity.

In addition, the CV experiment was also performed to study the kinetics of electron transport at the interface of modified electrodes. As shown in Fig. 4b, the experiment was carried out in 0.1 M KCl solution containing 5 mM of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- redox probe at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. The results demonstrate that the N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT/GCE exhibits greater redox behavior with a smaller peak-to-peak separation value (ΔE
p ) of about 76 mV. Whereas, ΔEp of MWCNT, N-CQD@SnO2/GCE, SnO2/GCE, and bare GCE are exhibited the values of 78, 130, 157, and 167 mV, respectively. The lowest ΔE
p and high current response at N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT/GCE demonstrate the fast redox reaction of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-, and it emphasizes that the hybrid nanocomposite offers the easy electron transport pathway at the electrode/electrolyte interface. It is due to the high conducting nature of the hybrid nanocomposite. The observed result is well comparable with the EIS experimental finding.

The electrochemical active surface area of the N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT/GCE was determined using various scan rates in 0.1 M KCl solution containing 5 mM of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- redox probe, as shown in Fig. 4c. When the scan rate is raised from 10 to 100 mV s−1, the redox peak current increases continuously. From the results, a plot between the square root of the scan rate and redox peak current, resulting in the linear plot shown in Fig. 4d. The active surface area is estimated using the Randles-Sevcik equation (Eq. 2), based on the slope of the linear plot.
(2) Ip = (2·69 × 105)n3/2D1/2v1/2AC*
Where I
p is redox peak current, n is transferred number of electrons (n = 1), D is diffusion coefficient of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- system, v is scan rate, A is electrochemical active surface area, and C* is a concentration of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- system. N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT/GCE has an estimated surface area of 0.235 cm2. Whereas, the electrochemical surface areas of bare GCE, SnO2/GCE, and N-CQD@SnO2/GCE were determined to be 0.078, 0.098, 0.126 cm2. All the foregoing results show that the N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT/GCE has a high surface area and a fast electron transfer capability. Thus, it indicates that it may be used as an effective electrode modifier for further electrochemical detection studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalytic performance of N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT/GCE towards CA determination
ce:section.ce:para: Using the CV technique, the electrochemical behavior of GA at the bare and modified electrodes was investigated. Fig. 5a shows the comparative CVs for the oxidation of GA at different modified electrodes. The bare GCE and SnO2/GCE have exhibited the very weak anodic peaks only, indicating the electrode process is not feasible at these electrodes, due to their weak conducting nature. Whereas, N-CQD@SnO2/GCE shows the noticeable anodic peak at 0.24 V with improved peak current intensity than bare and SnO2/GCE. Besides, MWCNT/GCE and N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT/GCE exhibit well separated three oxidation peaks and weak reduction peaks for GA, suggesting the partially reversible reaction of GA at these electrodes.

However, the N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT/GCE oxidizes the GA (O1, O2, and O3) at low oxidation potentials and improved oxidation current response than MWCNT/GCE (O1, O2, and O3), demonstrating the high electrocatalytic nature of the hybrid nanocomposite. This excellent feature can be attributed to the synergistic effect contributed by the individual constituent of the hybrid nanocomposite. For comparison, the oxidation current response of O2 at different electrodes is presented in the form of a bar diagram in Fig. 5b. It clearly depicts that the hybrid nanocomposite has a high electrocatalytic nature towards GA oxidation.

Fig. 5c shows the possible electrooxidation reactions at the surface of the modified electrode. The O1 for GA at the surface of N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT/GCE is caused by the loss of one electron from the hydroxyl group of the GA, resulting in the hydroxy radical as a product. Then the hydroxy radical loses one proton, which ends up with a stable semiquinone radical form (O2 at 0.19 V). The O3 is produced as a result of the transformation of the semiquinone radical into the quinone form. Among the three oxidation peaks, O2 is more predominant than O1 and O3. Moreover, weak reduction peaks were only observed, and they are negligible.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of scan rate and pH
ce:section.ce:para:
The various scan rate investigations are important for understanding the electrochemical reaction kinetics in the electrochemical cell. The influence of scan rate on the electrochemical response of GA was investigated at N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT/GCE using the CV technique in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7). As shown in Fig. 6a, the redox peak currents of GA (100 µM) rise as the scan rate increases from 10 to100 mV s−1, and also, the anodic peak current slightly shifts towards the positive side, while, the cathodic peaks shift to the negative side. The redox peak currents of GA were proportional to the scan rate (v) instead of the square root of the scan rate (v
2 ). As a result, it suggests that the redox process of GA at the modified electrode is a highly adsorption-controlled pathway. The linear connection between the anodic and cathodic peak current versus the scan rate (Fig. 6b) can be stated as Ipa = 0.8169 v + 14.3787 (µA, mV s−1) (R2 = 0.9955) and Ipa = −0.6548 v + 0.298 (µA, mV s−1) (R2 = 0.9996) respectively.

The effect of pH on the redox reaction of GA at the N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT/GCE was investigated using CV in 0.1 M PBS (pH 3, 5, 7, 9, and 11), and the findings are shown in Fig. 6c. The oxidation peak current of GA was found to be larger in pH 7 only, and the peak potentials (Ep) shift to more negative values as pH increases from 3 to 9, indicating the participation of protons during the GA oxidation [31]. Meanwhile, the peak O2 is more noticeable than peaks O1 and O3. Therefore, the relationship between the pH and Ep of peak O2 is further examined. Over the pH range of 3–11, the plot of pH vs. Ep shows a linear relationship (Fig. 6d), and the linear regression equation is Ep a (V) = −0.0564 pH + 0.703 (R2 = 0.9975). The measured slope value is extremely close to the predicted Nernstian value (59 mV/pH at 25 ºC), suggesting the involvement of an equal number of protons and electrons. The obtained results further confirm that the oxidation of GA takes place with the proposed reaction mechanism, as shown in Fig. 5c. Thus, pH 7 is an optimum value and is used for further electrochemical investigations.

Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) is a more sensitive measurement technique than the CV technique. The N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT/GCE’s sensitivity and detection limit towards the GA detection were calculated from DPV analysis. The DPVs of different concentrations of GA (0.05–360 µM) were performed using N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT/GCE in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0), and their results are shown in Fig. 7a. It was observed that increasing the concentration of GA caused a linear increase in anodic peak current. These results demonstrated that the N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT hybrid nanocomposite might provide improved electrocatalytic activity for GA sensing. Furthermore, a linear relationship was established between the anodic peak current and the concentration of GA (Fig. 7b). A linear regression equation may be stated as: 0.1075 [GA] (µM) + 119.62 with the correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9968. The sensitivity is estimated using the slope of the linear plot, which is 3.96 µA µM−1 cm−2 and the calculated limit of detection (LOD) value is 0.042 µM. The results of the experiments reveal that the N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT/GCE has excellent sensitivity and lower detection limits for the sensitive detection of GA. The analytical characteristics of the proposed sensor are then compared to those of other reported sensors, which are listed in Table 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interference studies, reproducibility, repeatability, and stability
ce:section.ce:para: DPV measurements were used to test the sensor’s interference stability in the presence of common interfering biomolecules such as glycine (Gly), sucrose (SU), oxalic acid (Ox), uric acid (UA), quercetin (Qu), ascorbic acid (AA), and caffeic acid (CA). The DPV profiles of N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT/GCE were recorded with 50 µM of GA in the presence of 100-fold higher concentrations of interferents. The results showed that in the presence of a high concentration of interfering molecules, the DPV current response of GA is slightly changed. Overall, less than 18% of the relative error percentage has occurred for the detection of GA using N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT/GCE in the presence of an excessive concentration of interferents. Notably, CA and Qu are alone increase the GA oxidation peak current ~17% and ~13% respectively.

Whereas the fluctuation in oxidation peak current response of GA was less than 11% for other interferents, as illustrated in the relative error bar diagram in Fig. 7c. The peak potential of GA, on the other hand, was unaffected by interferents. Thus, the results suggest that the N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT/GCE has a decent selectivity for the detection of GA. The sensors’ important criteria are reproducibility and repeatability. The reproducibility investigation was carried out with five separate N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT/GCE utilizing the DPV technique. The acquired findings are given in Fig. 8a, with an RSD value of about 1.8%, indicating that the N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT/GCE has high reproducibility.

Besides, the repeatability of the N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT/GCE was assessed by performing ten consecutive DPV experiments with GA of 100 µM in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7) at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 (Fig. 8b). From the experimental results, the RSD value was determined to be 2.7%. Likewise, the sensor’s storage stability was tested for up to 24 h, and the results depict that only 2.1% of the current response was reduced from the initial anodic peak current value Fig. 8c. The operational stability was also tested using CV experiments, which were carried out in 25 continuous cycles, as shown in Fig. 8d. There is no discernible fluctuation seen, indicating its good working stability. The results demonstrate that N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT/GCE can serve as a suitable electrode material for the sensitive determination of GA with good stability, selectivity, and reproducibility.

The suggested GA sensor was tested in a real sample, such as black tea, green tea, and oolong tea, to determine its suitability for practical use. For extraction, one pocket of tea sachet was steeped in 100 mL hot water for up to 15 min. Then the extract was diluted with PBS (pH 7), and then 10 mL of the diluted real sample was subjected to the DPV analysis. The concentration of GA present in the real sample was determined based on the constructed calibration curve (Fig. 7b). To validate the proposed sensor, a standard addition method with a known concentration of GA was also performed. By using the N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT/GCE, different known concentrations of standard GA were spiked to the real samples and then subjected to the DPV analysis. Along with the GA detection, the CA was also identified, but, it does not influence the detection of GA. Table 2 displays the acquired recovery outcomes, as well as the RSD values. Thus, the decent recovery findings indicate the N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT/GCE sensing platform’s suitability for the real sample analysis.

In summary, a new hybrid nanocomposite N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT was prepared and applied to an electrochemical application for the first time. The graphitic π conjugated structure of N-CQDs made a π-π interaction with MWCNTs and resulted in a stable hybrid nanocomposite. The presence of carbon nanomaterials (N-CQDs and MWCNTs) in the hybrid nanocomposite were synergistically contributed to enhancing the overall electrochemical performance towards GA detection. N-CQD@SnO2/MWCNT/GCE exhibited excellent electrochemical response compared to bare GCE and other counterparts. From the voltammetric studies, various parameters like scan rate, pH, and casting volume were optimized. Trace level determination of GA was performed using DPV. The proposed N-CQD/SnO2/MWCNT nanocomposite modified electrode has a fast and sensitive electrochemical response to GA sensing with good sensitivity (3.96 µA µM−1 cm−2), wide linear range (0.05–360 µM), and low detection limit (0.042 µM). Further, the proposed sensor has a decent anti-interference ability, repeatability, and reproducibility. Finally, the developed sensor showed appreciable recovery results from the real sample, thus it may be useful for industrial applications.

Ganesan Muthusankar: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Ramadhass Keerthika Devi: Conceptualization, Software, Writing – review & editing. Yu-Feng Huang: Data curation, Visualization. Shen-Ming Chen: Resources, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Gopalakrishnan Gopu: Resources, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge to University Grants Commision, India, for the financial assistance through contract no. UGC – BSR (25–1/2014 – 2015(BSR)/7 –88/2007(BSR), Dt.:15th-Oct-2015). The authors also acknowledge the financial support by the Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan through contract nos. MOST 109-2221-E182-031 and MOST 110-2113-M-027-003 .
